coredata.dc:title: Preparation of high performance blue-emitting carbon dots and their applications in anti-counterfeiting and tetracycline detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Hongxiang
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: In the realm of nanomaterials for security applications and environmental monitoring, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising candidates due to their unique optical properties and stability. This study presents a novel synthesis of nitrogen-doped blue-emitting fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) using malic acid and N,N-methylene bisacrylamide through a one-step solvothermal process, offering a high quantum yield of 30.1 %. The B-CDs exhibit remarkable photostability and are minimally affected by environmental factors such as pH, temperature, and ionic strength, making them suitable for various applications. We demonstrate the utility of B-CDs in two significant areas: as a security ink for anti-counterfeiting purposes, where they display stable and bright blue fluorescence under UV light, and as a sensitive and selective probe for the detection of tetracycline (TET), a widely used antibiotic. The B-CDs-based TET detection method showcases a low detection limit of 0.033 μmol·L⁻¹ and high recovery rates in spiked water samples, indicating its potential for real-world application in monitoring antibiotic residues. The findings not only contribute to the advancement of carbon dot technology but also have implications for food safety and security ink development, providing new avenues for the detection of contaminants and the protection against counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solvothermal method
Anti-counterfeiting encryption
Tetracycline

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of B-CDs. (a) TEM image of G-CDs. Inset is the corresponding particle size distribution at top right corner. (b) XRD spectrum. (c) FT-IR spectrum. (d) XPS survey spectrum. (e-g) High resolution C1s , O1s and N1s spectra, respectively.

Optical properties of B-CDs. ( a ) Three-dimensional EEM spectrum. (b) two-dimensional contour plot of the EEM. (c) Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum ( purple ), excitation spectrum ( blue ), emission spectrum ( cyan ). (d) Normallized normalized emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths.

Optical stability of B-CDs. ( a ) temperature ( b ) UV irradiation time ( c ) NaCl concentration on the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs ( d ) the fitting curve of integral fluorescence intensity to absorbance in different solvents, ( e ) quantum yields of B-CDs in different solvents.
(a) pH-dependent fluorescence intensity plot; (b) Zeta potential plot as a function of pH; (c-g) Malvern particle size distribution plots at different pH values.

Fluorescent anti-counterfeiting and encryption ink. (a) Pattern under natural light; (b) Pattern under UV lamp illumination; (c) Pattern re-illuminated after one week; (d) Fluorescence intensity after one month of storage; (e) Fluorescence intensity at 30 °C, 60 °C, and 80 °C.

TET (TET) detection. (a) Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs after the addition of antibiotics. (b) Ratio of fluorescence intensity of B-CDs after antibiotic addition to that before addition. (d) Ratio of fluorescence intensity of B-CDs after metal ion or organic dye addition to that before addition. (b) Anti-interference effect of B-CDs against metal ions and organic dyes. (e) Effect of TET concentration on the fluorescence of B-CDs. (f) Standard calibration curve fitted using the 1-F/F0
C relationship.
(a) Fluorescence lifetime spectra. (b) UV spectra of B-CDs, TET, and the B-CDs - TET complex. (c) UV absorption spectrum of TET and the optimal excitation spectrum of B-CDs.
Comparison of the proposed method with previously reported TET detection strategies.

head.ce:title: Preparation of high performance blue-emitting carbon dots and their applications in anti-counterfeiting and tetracycline detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bKey Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education), Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the realm of nanomaterials for security applications and environmental monitoring, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising candidates due to their unique optical properties and stability. This study presents a novel synthesis of nitrogen-doped blue-emitting fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) using malic acid and N,N-methylene bisacrylamide through a one-step solvothermal process, offering a high quantum yield of 30.1 %. The B-CDs exhibit remarkable photostability and are minimally affected by environmental factors such as pH, temperature, and ionic strength, making them suitable for various applications. We demonstrate the utility of B-CDs in two significant areas: as a security ink for anti-counterfeiting purposes, where they display stable and bright blue fluorescence under UV light, and as a sensitive and selective probe for the detection of tetracycline (TET), a widely used antibiotic. The B-CDs-based TET detection method showcases a low detection limit of 0.033 μmol·L⁻¹ and high recovery rates in spiked water samples, indicating its potential for real-world application in monitoring antibiotic residues. The findings not only contribute to the advancement of carbon dot technology but also have implications for food safety and security ink development, providing new avenues for the detection of contaminants and the protection against counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of carbon-based nanomaterial with a size smaller than 10 nm, first discovered in 2004 by Xu et al. during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotube fragments [1]. In recent years, CDs have gained significant attention due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, superior biocompatibility, and good water solubility [2]. Benefiting from their superior optical properties and abundant surface functional groups, CDs exhibit unique advantages in terms of detection efficiency, sensitivity, and selectivity, making them ideal alternatives to traditional quantum dots and organic dyes used in fluorescent probes. Compared with conventional fluorescent probes, CDs possess excellent dispersibility and efficient fluorescence emission capabilities. They can rapidly and uniformly mix with and respond to the samples under detection, enabling the completion of the detection process within a shorter timeframe and thus significantly enhancing detection efficiency. Moreover, the high quantum yield and low background interference of CDs endow them with remarkable sensitivity, conferring great application potential for CDs in the field of trace analysis. Additionally, the rich variety of functional groups on the surface of CDs contributes to their excellent selectivity. Through surface modification or functionalization, CDs can specifically interact with target substances, enabling accurate identification of these targets within complex sample matrices. Furthermore, the tunable excitation wavelength of CDs allows for the selection of an appropriate excitation wavelength based on the properties of the target substances, thereby reducing background interference and further improving detection selectivity. For example, Nandi et al. reported nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) with blue, green, and red emissions, which serve as potential nanoprobes for dual-mode detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid [3]. Davi and colleagues synthesized sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs via hydrothermal synthesis, demonstrating fluorescence properties sensitive to temperature and pH changes, where the fluorescence intensity increased with rising temperature and pH within a certain range, accompanied by a color shift [4]. Zhang et al. developed highly efficient red-emitting CDs with electron-withdrawing surface groups, achieving dual-photon imaging of mouse tumors and ear vasculature through blood circulation [5]. Through ongoing research, CDs have shown great potential in fields such as bioimaging [6,7], chemical sensing [8,9], environmental pollution control [10–12], and information encryption [13–15].

Tetracycline (TET), one of the most commonly used broad-spectrum antibiotics, has been widely applied in livestock farming, medicine, and other agricultural activities since its discovery, making a significant contribution to human survival and development [16]. However, overuse of TET leads to residual accumulation in animal tissues, posing potential health risks to consumers. To address this concern, several countries have established Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs) for TET in animal-derived food products. For instance, both China and the European Union have set the MRL for TET in milk at 225 nmol·L⁻¹, whereas the United States has established a higher limit of 676 nmol·L⁻¹ [17]. Currently, the prevalent methods for the effective detection of TET include chromatographic techniques [18], immunoassays [18], and fluorescent analysis methods [19–21]. Among these, chromatographic approaches often involve complex sample pretreatment procedures, are time-consuming, and may exhibit limited sensitivity. Immunoassays, on the other hand, are also time-intensive and incur high detection costs. In contrast, fluorescent detection methods offer advantages in terms of accuracy, rapidity, and high sensitivity. Consequently, fluorescent detection approaches have garnered significant interest and preference among researchers.

In this study, a series of nitrogen-containing substances were screened, and malic acid along with N,N-methylene bisacrylamide were ultimately selected as precursors to synthesize nitrogen-doped B-CDs via a solvothermal method. The B-CDs demonstrated a quantum yield of 30.1 %. Their photoluminescent properties and stability suggest their use as fluorescent ink for information encryption. Additionally, the high sensitivity and selectivity of B-CDs toward TET make them promising candidates for detecting TET residues in animal-derived foods, offering a novel approach to TET residue detection in complex food matrices.

Malic acid, N,N-methylene bisacrylamide, quinine sulfate, chloramphenicol (CHL), amoxicillin (AMX), gentamicin (GEN), kanamycin (KAN), ampicillin (AMP), ibuprofen (IBU), and TET were all of analytical grade and purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Ethanol, also of analytical grade, was procured from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. The water used in the experiments was self-prepared, double-distilled water. The ultrasonic cleaner was acquired from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd., and the thermostatic drying oven was obtained from Shanghai Lichen Technology Co., Ltd. UV–vis spectra were recorded using a 756 PC UV-visible spectrophotometer from Shanghai Metash Instruments Co., Ltd., while fluorescence spectra were measured with an F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer from Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd.

B-CDs were prepared by the solvothermal method using malic acid and N,N-methylenebisacrylamide as precursors and ethanol as the solvent. The specific operations are as follows: 0.40 g (3 mmol) of malic acid and 1.0 g (6 mmol) of N,N-methylenebisacrylamide were added to 10 ml of ethanol and sonicated for 10 min to ensure full dissolution. Then, the dispersion was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor and placed in an oven at 180 °C for 6 h. After the reactor naturally cooled to room temperature, the crude product was centrifuged at 10,000 r·min⁻¹ for 15 min to remove large - particle impurities. The supernatant was taken and dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut - off (MWCO) of 500 - 1000 Da for 72 h to obtain the B-CDs solution. The preparation process is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The effect of pH on the luminescent properties is investigated by preparing standard solutions with pH values ranging from 1 to 13 at room temperature. A 2 mL portion of the pH 7 solution is added to a cuvette, followed by the introduction of B-CDs, and the fluorescence intensity is adjusted to approximately 2000. The same procedure is then followed to measure the fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs in the standard solutions across the pH range of 1 to 13.

The effect of temperature on the luminescent properties of carbon dots is examined by adding B-CDs to ethanol, with fluorescence emission spectra measured at temperatures ranging from 5 °C to 60 °C, in 5 °C intervals. The optimal fluorescence intensity for each temperature is recorded.

Photobleaching resistance is assessed by adding 2 mL of ethanol solution to a cuvette, into which B-CDs are introduced. The fluorescence intensity is adjusted to around 2000. The cuvette is exposed to a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp positioned 5 cm away for 2 h, with the fluorescence emission spectrum measured every 20 min during irradiation. The maximum fluorescence intensity is recorded.

Salt resistance is evaluated by preparing a 1 mol·L⁻¹ NaCl solution at room temperature. Six centrifuge tubes are filled with 2 mL of ethanol solution and B-CDs, and the fluorescence intensity is adjusted to approximately 2000. Incremental amounts of 0, 200, 400, 600, 800, and 1000 μL of the 1 mol·L⁻¹ NaCl solution are added to each tube, and fluorescence detection is carried out.

Determination of solvent effects: Add 2 mL of each solvent, namely water (WAT), isopropanol (IPA), ethanol (EtOH), acetone (ACE), tetrahydrofuran (THF), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), into separate cuvettes. Subsequently, use a micropipette to transfer 40 μL of B-CDs solution into each cuvette. Perform fluorescence detection on these samples and record the optimal fluorescence intensities.

The morphology, structure, and composition of B-CDs are analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The TEM image shown in (Fig. 2a ) indicates poor dispersity of B-CD particles, with an average particle size of approximately 3.9 nm. (Fig. 2b) displays the XRD pattern of B-CDs, revealing a broad diffraction peak at 24.86°, which indicates the amorphous carbon structure of the B-CDs. The functional groups of B-CDs are characterized by FT-IR. As depicted in Fig. 2c, the strong absorption peak at 3738 cm⁻¹ is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH; the absorption peak at 3299 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the stretching vibration of NH; strong absorption peaks at 1700 cm⁻¹ and 1530 cm⁻¹ are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C=O and C=C, respectively; the absorption peak at 1254 cm⁻¹ represents the stretching vibration of CN; and the absorption peak at 893 cm⁻¹ is due to the out-of-plane bending vibration of CH. The results of FT-IR and XRD analysis indicate that the surface of B-CDs is rich in functional groups such as NH, OH, C=O, and C=C, which not only enhance the luminescence potential of B-CDs but also increase their hydrophilicity and stability. Additionally, the synthesis of B-CDs is based on dehydration condensation between carboxyl/hydroxyl and amine groups, followed by further carbonization [22]. The elemental composition of B-CDs is analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in (Fig. 2d), the XPS full-scan spectrum exhibits three prominent peaks corresponding to the elements O, N, and C, with a relative content ratio of 74.02:13.17:12.8 for C/N/O, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of B-CDs, shown in (Fig. 2e), presents three peaks: CC/C=C (284.6 eV), CN (285.7 eV), and C=O (288.1 eV). In (Fig. 2f), the N1s spectrum shows peaks at 400.00 eV and 399.53 eV, corresponding to CN and CN=C, respectively. The O1s spectrum in (Fig. 2g) reveals characteristic peaks at 531.64 eV and 533.42 eV, which correspond to C=O and CO, respectively.

The optical properties of B-CDs were investigated using excitation-emission matrices (EEM), UV–Vis absorption spectra, and fluorescence emission spectra. (Fig. 3a ) presents a three-dimensional EEM spectrum, showing a distinct peak, which indicates an optimal combination of excitation and emission wavelengths for the B-CDs. (Fig. 3b) displays a two-dimensional contour plot of the EEM, with the optimal excitation wavelength ranging between 360 and 390 nm, and the emission wavelength extending from approximately 380 nm to 500 nm. (Fig. 3c) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (UV), excitation spectrum (ex), and emission spectrum (em). The UV spectrum features a broad absorption band between 300 and 400 nm, likely originating from π→π* electronic transitions involving -C=O, C=C, and C=N bonds [23]. The optimal excitation wavelength was identified as 377 nm, with the optimal emission wavelength at 446 nm, and minimal overlap between the two (Fig. 3d) depicts normalized emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths. A redshift in the emission peak is observed as the excitation wavelength increases, indicating the excitation-dependent nature of the B-CDs, a characteristic of carbon quantum dots.

The optical stability of the B-CDs was evaluated by testing their fluorescence intensity under varying conditions, including temperature, salt concentration, continuous UV lamp exposure time, different solvents, and various pH environments. As illustrated in (Fig. 4a ), when ethanol was used as the solvent and the temperature was increased from 5 °C to 60 °C, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs remained nearly constant, indicating their insensitivity to temperature fluctuations. Upon continuous UV lamp irradiation of the B-CDs for 2 h, with fluorescence intensity monitored at 20 min intervals, the results depicted in (Fig. 4b) revealed a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity over the 120 min irradiation period, yet the overall intensity remained stable, suggesting good photobleaching resistance of B-CDs.

From (Fig. 4c), it is evident that as the NaCl concentration increased from 0 to 1000 mM, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs remained largely unchanged, demonstrating their excellent salt resistance. Furthermore, we investigated the luminescent properties of B-CDs in different solvents by plotting the calibration curves of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance for each solvent (Fig. 4d). Using quinine sulfate (REF) solution as a standard (with a quantum yield of 54 % [24], the quantum yields of B-CDs in various solvents were calculated, as shown in (Fig. 4e). The highest quantum yield of approximately 30.1 % was observed for B-CDs in acetone (ACE), followed by ethanol (EtOH) and tetrahydrofuran (THF). Relatively lower quantum yields were recorded in water (WAT) and isopropanol (IPA). Collectively, these results underscore the exceptional environmental stability and relatively high quantum yields of the synthesized B-CDs, particularly noteworthy in certain organic solvents. These attributes render B-CDs promising candidates for diverse applications, including sensing, imaging, and optoelectronic devices.

As observed in (Fig. 5a ), the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs remains relatively stable within the pH range of 2 to 11. However, at pH values of 1, 12, and 13, a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity is noted. From (Fig. 5b), it is evident that as the pH decreases from 2 to 1, the Zeta potential changes from 16.9 mV to 12.2 mV, accompanied by an increase in particle size (as depicted in Fig. 5d-c). This indicates that under strongly acidic conditions, the functional groups on the surface of B-CDs undergo protonation, thereby altering their surface charge distribution and leading to a certain degree of aggregation of B-CDs. Meanwhile, as the pH increases from 11 to 12 and 13, the Zeta potential shifts from −7.7 mV to −7.6 mV and −3.3 mV, respectively, with a substantial increase in particle size (as shown in Fig. 5e-g). This suggests that under strongly alkaline conditions, the electrostatic repulsion between B-CDs particles is diminished, resulting in aggregation or precipitation. These findings demonstrate that B-CDs exhibit good dispersibility and stability within the pH range of 2 to 11, indicating their broad applicability across various environmental conditions.

In recent years, due to the escalating prevalence of counterfeiting activities, anti-counterfeiting technologies have garnered increasing attention from renowned product enterprises worldwide as a preventive measure. To meet this demand, various anti-counterfeiting materials and technologies have been developed. Yakunin [25] developed a monochromatic security label encoded with radiative lifetimes using perovskite nanocrystals, which exhibit favorable fluorescence intensity and tunable emission wavelengths but suffer from poor stability. Wang's [26] team investigated silicon quantum dots with excellent biocompatibility and photostability for integrated circuit anti-counterfeiting applications; however, their limited fluorescence intensity restricts their broader utilization. Gou [27] prepared a stimuli-responsive smart luminescent material by co-loading green-emitting CDs and red fluorescent Eu-MOFs onto aminoclay. While MOFs offer good chemical stability and tunable pore structures, their fluorescent signals are susceptible to environmental influences. CDs have emerged as a vital component in the anti-counterfeiting field owing to their low cost, ease of preparation, and superior optical properties. B-CDs, abundant in hydrophilic functional groups on their surface, enable inkjet printing of CD-based inks onto paper with excellent adhesion. Furthermore, the remarkable optical properties of B-CDs endow them with significant potential for anti-counterfeiting applications.

Fig. 6 illustrates the results of painting and writing using fluorescent ink formulated with B-CDs. The outline was first drawn with a ballpoint pen, followed by filling with the prepared fluorescent ink. Under daylight, only the traces of the ballpoint pen are visible on the paper (Fig. 6a), whereas under UV lamp illumination, a vivid blue color emerges (Fig. 6b). Upon re-examination of the pattern after one week of storage, it remains clear and bright (Fig. 6c). The fluorescence intensity of the ink exhibits minimal change after one month of storage under natural light (Fig. 6d). Additionally, as depicted in (Fig. 6e), the fluorescence intensity of the ink shows slight variations at 30 °C and 60 °C, with more pronounced changes observed at 80 °C. These CDs offer a novel approach to enhancing the stability and security of anti-counterfeiting and encryption technologies.

Antibiotics [28] are chemical substances capable of inhibiting the normal growth of other cells, exhibiting antibacterial and antiviral activities. They are commonly employed to treat various diseases caused by bacteria and viruses. However, due to their persistence, accumulative properties, and potential to transform into other chemical substances, antibiotic residues can pose health risks to humans, including carcinogenesis, reduced immunity, and drug resistance. In this study, we investigated the detection of several commonly available antibiotics and found that B-CDs exhibit high selectivity for TET detection (Fig. 7a-b). To further validate the selectivity of B-CDs, we selected several common environmental pollutants, including heavy metal ions and organic dyes, as shown in (Fig. 7c and d) It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs remained largely unchanged in the presence of other pollutants but decreased upon the addition of TET, indicating promising applications of B-CDs in real-world environmental monitoring. From (Fig. 7f ), it is evident that within the TET concentration range of 0.04–0.14 μmol·L⁻¹, the fluorescence quenching effect of TET on B-CDs is positively correlated with its concentration. B-CDs demonstrate excellent selectivity and sensitivity in detecting TET, offering a novel approach for TET residue detection in complex environments.

To explore the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, we further investigated the interaction mechanism between B-CDs and TET through UV - vis absorption spectra, fluorescence emission spectra, and fluorescence lifetime diagrams. Currently, common quenching mechanisms include static quenching (SQ), dynamic quenching (DQ), photo - induced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and inner filter effect (IFE). As shown in (Fig. 8a ), the introduction of TFT reduced the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs from 4.12 ns to 3.27 ns. Meanwhile, from (Fig. 8b), it can be seen that the absorption spectrum of the B-CDs solution did not show a significant shift after the introduction of TET. This indicates that there is a certain interaction between TET and B-CDs, which may be electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bonding, or π - π stacking, but not static quenching. Moreover, the dynamic quenching rate constant can be calculated using the following formula:
k q
=
1
τ 0
(
1 τ
−
1
τ 0
)
where
τ 0
is the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs and τ is the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs + TET. The dynamic quenching rate constant between B-CDs and TET is 1.2 × 10 10 mol⁻¹·L·s⁻¹, which is close to the diffusion - controlled limit. This indicates that the collision between TET and the excited state of B-CDs is very efficient, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of B-CDs in detecting TET is mainly dynamic quenching. There is a partial overlap between the emission peak of B-CDs and the absorption peak of TET (Fig. 8c), which may lead to FRET and further intensify the fluorescence quenching.

In this work, nitrogen-doped B-CDs were successfully synthesized via a solvothermal approach, utilizing malic acid and N,N'-methylenebisacrylamide as precursors. The prepared B-CDs exhibit superior optical properties, with a notable quantum yield of up to 30.1 %. Moreover, these B-CDs demonstrate excellent optical stability against variations in salt concentration, ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, pH levels, temperature fluctuations, and exposure to diverse solvents. This robustness enables them to maintain stable fluorescence emission under a broad spectrum of complex environmental conditions, thereby providing a solid foundation for their practical applications. Under UV illumination, B-CDs emit intense blue fluorescence while exhibiting good stability and heat resistance, making them suitable candidates for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications. Furthermore, B-CDs display high selectivity and sensitivity in detecting TET, with a detection limit as low as 0.033 μM and a linear detection range spanning from 0.04 μM to 0.14 μM. These attributes render B-CDs promising as fluorescent probes for the detection of antibiotic residues in aqueous solutions. The reliability of this detection method has been further validated through spiked recovery experiments with TET. Looking ahead, the development of portable B-CDs-based detection devices holds significant potential for enabling rapid on-site testing, thereby expanding their practical applications. Concurrently, evaluating the biocompatibility and toxicity of fluorescent CDs constitutes a critical area for future research endeavors, which could further broaden their application domains. It is anticipated that through continued research efforts, the applications of B-CDs across various fields can be advanced, offering innovative perspectives and methodologies for technological advancements in related disciplines.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors..
Hongxiang Liu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Shaogui Wu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.
